But yeah, but how's it going?
Pretty good.
We, you know, I've been, I wear all the hats, right?
So this week I've been wearing HR hat and we hired three people.
So it's like getting them on boarded and ready to go.
Congrats.
Are they more on the, like, go-to-market side?
Everybody's engineering right now.
Ah, builders.
Yeah.
That's cool.
We, Hey, Gloria.
Bridget, who I think you've met, handles all that.
I think we just have, like, she's done such an amazing job, like, setting up this pipeline of people.
And I feel like all the, all the funny videos, you know, we've been posting has, has helped just, like, a lot of inbound, too, which has been really nice.
That's cool.
Yeah, you guys have been growing, like, crazy.
our Bye.
Bye
Yeah, we're definitely signing more people, still like, you know, trying to figure out our model and how to like consistently serve everyone, but yeah, pretty good.
Yeah, but I guess the purpose was just to kind of pick your brain on the event you guys did, because I think we're thinking about doing kind of a, probably a smaller one with Webflow.
Even in this meeting, I think we like scaled it down even a little bit more, but essentially about, you know, like AEO, GEO, and all those things.
So just wanted to learn from like your event, because unfortunately none of us had the chance to go, but it looked like it was a pretty big success.
Yeah, we did record it, and both the panel and the lightning talks are on our, on the website, Future of Marketing SF, if you want to like scroll through and check it out.
But I'll give you the overview.
Basically, we wanted to build more brand awareness amongst B2B CMOs, and we got invited to participate by XYZ.
who is our investor.
They wanted to run the show.
So they invited us, they invited Graphite, and eventually they invited Sequel to sponsor as well.
And they took most of the ownership of getting the venue.
They had a relationship with the battery, and they also had relationships with some of the speakers.
So we helped source some of the speakers, and then we got to participate in a lightning talk.
So the format of the event was, the main part of it was the panel.
And the idea was to have one moderator, moderate a panel of three, maybe four CMOs, and have, you know, kind of riff off each other.
We invited a bunch of people to speak, and then they all said, yeah, so we ended up with five very quickly.
So, and normally it's not five CMOs, usually it's three CMOs and one VP of marketing or like a rev ops person.
And a finance person, it kind of makes it interesting, but it ended up being five CMOs.
So we just went with it.
And that was the main draw, I think, between the CMOs and the venue.
That was the main draw for the event.
The venue was, we rented out the penthouse at the Battery.
So Battery is already hard to get.
Did you have the dinner in the same space?
Yeah, same me.
What was the vibe?
Was it, like, more of a stand-up, like, hors d'oeuvres type Yeah, it wasn't, like, sit down.
It was more, like, there were various buffets set up, so you could go get a plate of, they had, like, a kebab station, they had a pizza station, they had some pasta thing.

And so you can go, like, find food and then bring it back to, like, it felt like a backyard table, different seating areas with fireplaces, and kind of just sit around somebody and then talk to them and find out, you know, what's going on.
So that's how the dinner ran.
Got it.
Did you guys consider doing like a morning half day versus like an evening half day at all?
We wanted to get the right people in the door.
We wanted it for sure to be on weekdays, Monday through Thursday, and Monday is pretty bad too, so Tuesday through Thursday.
And we also thought that the people we're bringing in are probably really busy, they have full day of meetings and stuff.
And so being able to, the time they could probably carve off is like four to seven, right?
That's the end of a day on a Thursday is probably easiest to duck out of work and go to a thing.
So that's, I think it's a sweet spot of timing.
I would recommend doing that.
It's easy to leave early, right?
And then it doesn't go too late.
But you guys have done breakfasts too, so I've seen the other example of that.
do the breakfast, but that does end a little bit earlier.
Yeah, the difference is, I think, with breakfast, people...
They'll have to rush off because they have things to do.
And with this, like, the trailing end is, like, people stayed until it closed.
People, like, maybe 10, 15 people stayed until 10 when it closed because they got really deep in conversation and made connections.
And I think that's what people want.
They want to make connections that are real.
They want to make friends.
They don't want to be pitched at or sold to.
You guys do a similar method with your events, too.
Yeah.
And your event was, like, 100 people or over 100?
We had, our cap was 100.
I think we had 130 or so registered and, like, 110 came.
Oh, wow.
Okay.
And it was free or it like, invite only but free?
Invite only but free and not necessarily public.
Like, we posted on LinkedIn and stuff.
But we ended up getting maybe 200-something, 50 signups, and we rejected a lot of people.
So, at the beginning, we accepted a few
So that there would be some people there.
then we started getting pickier and pickier about the titles and the levels and stuff.
And eventually, you know, as we got to the last 20 seats, it was like, well, it's got to be, you know, target market for all four of us or else.
Yeah.
And who did the, like, it sounded like the VC did the have, like, did the initial organization of it?
Like, did they do most of the heavy lifting and lighting or is it pretty?
No, they did the organization with the venue because that's quite a bit of work.
You have to, you know, schedule it and figure out what IT needs you have.
There was a video recording.
did live streaming with SQL.
So there was, like, three or four video camera operators, a live stream operator, and then this catering and, like, all the scheduling of when things come and go.
So they handled all that.
We, the rest of us did the invitations.
So we kind of tag-teamed the CMO invites and the invitations were everybody sent out invitations.
And all the partners had access to the Luma, so they would go and approve people they really wanted.
And we just had an agreement on kind of approving people that made sense for the others, too.
Like, we only want B2B to be, we don't want B2B to see.
So, you know, I think people were mostly cool about that.
And then it was helpful to have one person be in charge of just reviewing everybody else.
So if people signed up and we didn't know them, it was my job to go and research them and either approve or reject.
And the rejection was really more of a deflection to say, you know, we're pretty full and it's waitlist.
You're on the waitlist.
We'll see if Spot opens up.
But for now, there's a live stream.
You can join that.
So a good strategy, I think, was set the cap really low so that you quickly get to it.
And then there's waitlist for everyone.
So.
Makes sense.
I don't worry about other questions.
Oh, what was the rough cost of the whole thing?
Yeah, so.
So, I think it ended up being $40,000.
Okay.
Wait, so per person or just for the whole thing you guys split it?
No, I think it was 50.
Okay, so it was $50,000.
Graphite paid 10, we paid 10, and XYZ handled 30 or so.
And then we got a 5K sponsorship from SQL.
Okay, got it.
Yeah.
Seems a lot cheaper.
I think we were like, yeah, I guess it really is like venue dependent.
Yeah, honestly, what made it really expensive was the food.
Defeating all those people, that raised the cost a lot.
And then the AV was quite a bit too because of all the people and the equipment.
Like we ended up doing a confidence monitor.
We ended up doing, you know, piping and sound for music.
Oh, we lost you.
I don't know, still here.
Sorry.
Sorry.
Yeah, so there's just a few of those things made it more expensive, but otherwise, you know, it's not that expensive.
Outside of the venue, basically, our costs were, outside of venue and IT, our costs were badges, swag bags, maybe like $2,000 total.
Yeah, venue is usually what takes up, venue and AV is always what takes up the most.
What kind of, do you call, what kind of seating you guys had in there?
Yeah.
Just theater that you had inside the space where the presentation was?
Yeah, we had theater seating, and it was packed, so we encouraged people to sit, like, close to each other, and then I was aiming for a few people having to stand, so, and that's always fine.
I think if, like, you know, 5% of the people have to stand, they're kind of okay with that.
But, yeah, it was theater.
For the panel, we did a round of lightning talks.
So that was kind of creative.
The lightning talks were five minutes each.
And so you got to talk about some cool new technology in the space.
needed to not be pitchy and be actually informative and teach people things.
So five minutes for each company that did that.
And XYZ had a few companies in their portfolio that they wanted to speak.
Tiny, tiny companies were doing really cool stuff.
And then we did a 15-minute break.
And then we went into a hour-long panel.
And for both of these, the seating was theater-style.
And right when the panel was done, we started moving the seats apart.
And we made rounds for roundtables.
And then it just, with the audience of CMOs, they did not want to do roundtables.
So we ended up scrapping the roundtables entirely.
We had moderators selected.
We had topics.
But people were just so chatty and, like, so into it.
And having dinner and having great conversations.
we were trying to...
Get people to get back together and reassemble.
we thought, you know, let's just let them enjoy that.
nobody complained about not being able to do the roundtables, except for the moderators that we'd asked to present and prepare.
So we had to navigate that a little bit.
Makes sense.
That was, I mean, you answered pretty much all my other questions.
Just been going over everything.
So I don't have any other questions to you, Jason.
You might be traveling, might be moving.
I'll add one more thing while, oh, Jason dropped off.
Yeah.
What also helps if it's like high touch events like this is doing an Uber voucher.
I don't know if you've done that before, but it's great.
Okay.
To get people in.
Uber or did you do a Lyft voucher?
Because Uber is pretty difficult when it comes to Uber.
Uber is so difficult.
difficult.
We couldn't figure it out for this event either.
I've done it in the past, but because we have an Uber Eats plan, it wouldn't let us do it.
It was so complicated.
I think probably I would do Lyft in the future, but some kind of thing.
This time, it was interesting.
Everybody came and our attendance rate was really high and we didn't have any issues.
Nobody got lost.
I think we had a lot of communication leading up to the event.
was like, thank you for registering, confirming your registration.
Okay, it's in a week.
Like, hey, I just want to confirm that you're still coming because there's a wait list now.
Are you still going to be there?
And people said, yes, I'm still going to be there.
Please hold my spot.
And I said, okay, I'm expecting you.
Here's how to get in.
And I think people did appreciate the clear communication.
few people brought it up.
Yeah.
Did you also send a calendar invite?
Luma does it automatically, so we didn't do it.
Okay.
Yeah.
I do like calendar invites.
It's nicer to do it yourself because you could see if they accept or not.
With Luma, it's like hard to see if it went out.
And, you know, are people, I don't know.
Yeah, yeah.
Don't love Luma.
I also had hid the attendee list on Luma until the last week.
And then I opened it to just the people who were in.
Okay.
All right.
So was the venue that you thought was a draw?
Was the speakers?
Okay.
Cool.
Oh, one more question.
So really, it just ended up being like, what, two or three hours worth of content?
Yeah, because I think it started at 5.30 is when we actually started.
then 4.30.
4.30 is when doors opened.
I think 5.45 is when we got started.
Then we did like 15 minutes of lightning talks.
was really fast.
20 minutes of lightning talks.
then 6, 6 to 7.
So yeah, the content was about an hour and a half.
Okay.
All right.
That is good.
Cool.

Yeah, and also, like, I think it was good to put the panel after the lightning talks, because then people came and showed up for the panel, and they were, basically, everybody was there in time, because people come, like, an hour or too late, but, you know.
Always.
I mean, whether it's in the evening or in the morning, you're always going to have those people who just stroll in whenever they're ready.
Yeah.
You can, especially because of traffic.
You know, one other thing that helped a lot is hiring a photographer on site, and then putting all the photos in a Google Drive, and sharing it out to all the attendees immediately after.
So, what I noticed is people take a photo, and then they write a LinkedIn post about it.
At least with this crowd, like, they want to talk about it on LinkedIn, what they're invited to, what they did.
So, we gave them all the things.
Here's the picture, here's some, you know, here's everybody else that you met at the event, and they're LinkedIn, so you can connect with all of them.
And really...
Encourage them to be on LinkedIn.
I think we got maybe 10 people posting about it on LinkedIn.
So we probably got a lot of eyeballs on the event afterwards as well.
Weirdly enough, in most of the posts, people didn't know who sponsored it.
Like they tagged, you know, there was four companies and they tagged like two of the four mostly.
So I think we probably could have done better at just reminding them.
But, you know, it's a start.
Yeah.
I mean, it sounds like it was a successful start for the event.
I did look into using the battery and holy moly.
They're expensive.
It's pricey.
Yeah, but it's so crazy.
It was so, for me, I've done DemandGen for many years.
This was the easiest event I've ever promoted.
It just filled up really fast.
And then there was a wait list.
And every other event I do, I'm like sending out emails and trying to get the numbers up.
And so I credit that to either or both the five speakers having brands.
People know, and they wanted to see them speak, or, and, or the battery.
Yeah.
So, if you do Webflow at the battery, or somebody, somewhere similar, really, like, exclusive space, then I think you'll get people to come.
Yeah, I think so, too.
Cool.
Yep, I think that was very helpful.
Thank you so much for your time, and...
Yeah, thank you, Gloria.
Thank you.
Good luck.
Let me know how I can be helpful.
Will do.
Thank you.
Bye.
Thanks.
